articles.title: Brickbat: Cop Out
articles.content:
科罗拉多州一个陪审团裁定前新泽西州投资经理罗伯特·戴尔获得2400万美元赔偿，此前陪审团认定，一名警察侦探错误地逮捕了他，称这起案件与戴尔的儿子卷入2022年的一起枪击事件有关。根据诉讼内容，帕克警察局的夏农·布鲁克巴赫侦探对戴尔为其子聘请律师感到不满，尽管戴尔当时身处另一州，并且已告诉其儿子配合警方调查，她仍多次向戴尔施压索要信息。布鲁克巴赫后来指控戴尔帮助隐藏枪支，尽管警方在案发现场立即找到了枪支，但几周后检察官就撤销了指控。这次逮捕永久性地剥夺了戴尔从事金融行业的执照，实际上终结了他的职业生涯。虽然陪审团裁定支持戴尔，但布鲁克巴赫现已退休，很可能没有足够资金来支付这笔赔偿金，这意味着戴尔可能永远无法获得大部分赔偿。

文章Brickbat：警察失职首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
A Colorado jury awarded former New Jersey investment manager Robert Dial $24 million after finding that a police detective wrongfully arrested him in connection with a 2022 shooting involving Dial's son. According to the lawsuit, Detective Shannon Brukbacher of the Parker Police Department was upset that Dial hired a lawyer for his son, and she repeatedly pressed him for information, even though Dial was in another state and had told his son to cooperate with police. Brukbacher later accused Dial of helping hide a gun, even though officers found it immediately at the scene, and prosecutors dropped the charges just a few weeks later. The arrest permanently cost Dial his license to work in the financial industry, effectively ending his career. Although the jury ruled in Dial's favor, Brukbacher is retired and likely does not have enough money to personally pay the judgment, meaning Dial will probably never collect most of the judgment.

The post Brickbat: Cop Out appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: The Foreign-Born Heroes Who Chose America
articles.content:
当美国最高法院大法官尼尔·戈萨奇最近将美国描述为一个“信条国家”——一个由理念而非血统定义的国家——这一说法引发了争论。但戈萨奇在这一点上是正确的：美国建立在《独立宣言》的理念之上，而非血统之上。

自建国以来，美国吸引并同化来自世界各地人民的独特能力证明了这一论点。从美国革命至今，外国出生的人们为美国而战——不是因为他们与本土出生的美国人有血缘关系，而是因为他们相信美国的个人权利原则，并选择为之而战。

或许最著名的外国出生的革命者例子就是拉法耶特侯爵。他在回忆录中提到，1775年的一次晚宴上，他听到英王乔治三世的兄弟嘲笑美国革命的理想，于是立即决定了解更多并加入这一事业。“我的心已投身其中，我只想将自己的旗帜与革命者并肩作战，”他后来写道。拉法耶特19岁时乘船来到这片土地，并参加了包括1781年约克镇战役在内的许多战斗，发挥了关键作用。

拉法耶特是在迷恋上独立事业后选择为美国而战的。1778年，他写道：“我一听说美国就爱上了她；我一知道她为自由而战，就渴望为她流血；而我能在任何时候、任何地方为她服务的时刻，将是我一生中最幸福的时刻。”

他并不是唯一一个对这个国家怀有深深敬意的外国人，尽管他与这个国家没有任何先前的联系。

普鲁士出生的军官冯·施托本男爵被认为是美国军队的奠基人之一。受本杰明·富兰克林招募，这位经验丰富的军官加入大陆军以提升军队的专业化水平。被任命为临时总监察长后，面对军队的糟糕状况，冯·施托本制定了一套训练计划，极大地提高了美军的作战能力。他的作用对美国胜利至关重要，他制定的军事规章一直帮助军事行动直到1814年。

尽管他与美国人语言不通，但冯·施托本与他们共享对自由的热爱。“为一个致力于捍卫其权利和自由的可敬国家服务的荣誉，是我来到这片大陆的唯一动机，”他在1777年写给国会的信中说道。他对乔治·华盛顿写道：“我最大的抱负是尽我所能为您的国家服务，并通过为您的自由而战来赢得美国公民的称号。”在各方面都是美国人，只是在纸面上还不是的冯·施托本于1784年成为归化美国公民。

波兰出生的士兵卡齐米日·普瓦斯基就没那么幸运了，他在34岁时死于萨凡纳围城战。普瓦斯基自己的话揭示了他加入革命事业的动机：“我来到这里，因为自由正在这里被捍卫，我要为它服务，为它生或为它死，”他写信给乔治·华盛顿。

由于他的军事专长和对自由的承诺而被招募，普瓦斯基最终指挥了普瓦斯基军团，这是一支由美国和外国新兵组成的骑兵部队。像冯·施托本一样，普瓦斯基不会说英语，却因对这个国家的承诺而赢得了“美国骑兵之父”和“自由战士”的称号。

外国人保卫美国的模式在独立战争后并未停止。移民们在此后的每一场战争中都参战，至今仍在如此（即使在那些往往与他们入伍时要捍卫的理想关系不大的冲突中，即使政府正在努力驱逐其中一些人）。根据最近的一份国会报告，截至2026年2月，估计有5万名非美国公民在武装部队服役，约有12.5万名曾在现役部队服役的非公民退伍军人居住在美国。（这一数字不包括已成为公民的外国出生的退伍军人。）

其中一位服役者是阿尔弗雷多·拉斯孔·维拉斯奎兹，一位墨西哥出生的退伍军人，最初非法进入该国，在越南战争中获得荣誉勋章。他最终成为美国公民。“我内心深处一直是个美国人，”当被问及为什么在成为公民之前就志愿参军去越南时，他说。他将自己描述为“出生时是墨西哥人，选择成为美国人”。

所有这些案例中的关键线索是，它们反映了一种对美国价值观的第一手、有意识的钦佩，而不是对强加给他们的继承事业的牺牲或盲从。这些人明白他们有所收获：他们为想要生活的国家而战，知道美国革命的个人权利原则使他们想要享受的自由成为可能。他们的美国精神和承诺来自于对这些原则的理解，而非“种族”或部落义务。

正如戈萨奇所说，美国是一个建立在《独立宣言》理念基础上的国家，这些理念在250年来说服了全世界数百万人。在阵亡将士纪念日受到表彰的外国出生的男女做出了数百万移民每天都在做出的选择：拥抱一个由个人权利而非血统定义的国家。

本文最初发表于《橙县纪事报》。

《选择美国的外国出生英雄》一文首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
When Justice Neil Gorsuch recently described America as a "creedal nation"—one defined by ideas rather than bloodlines—it sparked a debate. But Gorsuch is right about this: America is based on the ideas of the Declaration of Independence, not heritage.

America's unique capacity to attract and assimilate people from around the world has proven this thesis since its founding. From the American Revolution to today, foreign-born individuals have fought for America—not because they shared a lineage with native-born Americans, but because they believed in America's principle of individual rights and chose to defend it.

Perhaps the most famous example of a foreign-born revolutionary is that of the Marquis de Lafayette. In his memoirs, he recalls hearing King George III's brother mock the ideals of the American Revolution at a 1775 dinner—and immediately resolving to learn more and join the cause. "My heart was enlisted and I thought only of joining my colors to those of the revolutionaries," he later wrote. The Marquis sailed to these shores at age 19 and fought in many battles, including Yorktown in 1781, where he played a crucial role.

Lafayette chose to fight for America after he became enamored with the cause for independence. In 1778, he wrote: "The moment I heard of America I loved her; the moment I knew she was fighting for freedom I burnt with a desire of bleeding for her; and the moment I shall be able to serve her, at any time, or in any part of the world, will be the happiest of my life." 

He wasn't the only foreigner who felt this profound reverence for this country, even though he didn't have any prior connection to it.

Prussian-born military officer Baron von Steuben is regarded as one of the fathers of the United States military. Recruited by Benjamin Franklin, the veteran officer joined the Continental Army to professionalize its ranks. Appointed temporary inspector general and dismayed at the state of the Army, von Steuben developed a training program that radically improved the Americans' abilities to fight the war. His role was instrumental in the American victory, and the military regulations he developed continued assisting military efforts until 1814.

Though he did not share a language with the Americans, von Steuben shared their devotion to freedom. "The honor of serving a respectable Nation, engaged in the noble enterprise of defending its rights and Liberty, is the only motive that brought me over to this Continent," he wrote to Congress in 1777. To George Washington, he wrote: "The object of my greatest ambition is to render your Country all the Services in my Power, and to deserve the title of a Citizen of America by fighting for the Cause of your Liberty." An American in every respect except on paper when he fought, von Steuben became a naturalized U.S. citizen in 1784.

Polish-born soldier Casimir Pulaski didn't get such an honor, as he died during the Siege of Savannah at 34. Pulaski's own words reveal his motivation to join the Revolutionary cause: "I came here, where freedom is being defended, to serve it, and to live or die for it," he wrote to George Washington.

Recruited for his military expertise and commitment to freedom, Pulaski eventually took command of the Pulaski Legion, a cavalry unit composed of American and foreign recruits. Like von Steuben, Pulaski didn't speak English, yet earned the titles of "Father of the American Cavalry" and "Soldier of Liberty" for his commitment to this country.

The pattern of foreigners defending America didn't stop after the Revolutionary War. Immigrants have fought in every war since then, and do so to this day (even in conflicts that often have little to do with the ideals they enlisted to defend, and even as the government moves to deport some of them). Per a recent congressional report, an estimated 50,000 non-U.S. citizens were serving in the Armed Forces as of February of 2026, and there are approximately 125,000 non-citizen veterans who previously served in active duty living in the U.S. (This figure doesn't count foreign-born veterans who have become citizens.)

Among those who served is Alfredo Rascon Velasquez, a Mexican-born veteran who originally entered the country illegally and won the Medal of Honor during the Vietnam War. He eventually became a U.S. citizen. "I was always an American in my heart," he said when asked why he had volunteered to join and go to Vietnam before he was even a citizen. He describes himself as "Mexican by birth, American by choice."

A key thread in all these cases is that they reflect a first-hand, purposeful admiration for American values, not sacrifice or blind devotion for an inherited cause imposed on them. These individuals understood that they had something to gain: They fought for the country they wanted to live in, knowing that America's revolutionary principle of individual rights enabled the freedoms they wanted to enjoy. Their Americanism and commitment come from understanding these principles, not from "ethnicity" or tribal duty.

America is, as Gorsuch said, a nation based on the ideas of the Declaration of Independence, and these ideas have persuaded millions across the world for 250 years. The foreign-born men and women honored on Memorial Day made a version of a choice millions of immigrants make every day: to embrace a country defined by individual rights, not bloodlines.

This column was first published in the Orange County Register.

The post The Foreign-Born Heroes Who Chose America appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Japan's Smoking Bans Make a Lot More Sense Than America's
articles.content:
走进东京三家不同的酒吧，你可能会体验到三种完全不同的氛围：一家烟雾缭绕，一家角落里有个嗡嗡作响的密封玻璃吸烟室，还有一家完全无烟。

在监管框架内，选择往往取决于店主和顾客双方：经营者决定他们想经营什么样的空间，顾客则决定自己偏好哪种环境。相比之下，美国大部分地区都由州或地方法律全面禁止室内吸烟。大约82.4%的美国人受到工作场所、餐厅和酒吧100%无烟规则的覆盖，这在很大程度上将决定权从市场中移除。

与许多其他国家一样，日本对吸烟的监管非常严格。2020年修订的《健康增进法》对公园、政府大楼、医院和大多数工作场所等公共场所的室内吸烟引入了重大限制。

在日本许多城市，街头吸烟也受到限制。你不能简单地站在户外或走在人行道上点燃香烟。吸烟者必须寻找指定区域——用玻璃或塑料隔板隔开的小型吸烟站，配备烟灰缸。在这些区域外吸烟可能导致高达30万日元（1890美元）的罚款。

乍一看，日本的制度似乎比美国的规则更严格，美国禁止室内吸烟但通常容忍户外吸烟。但日本的法律留下了令人惊讶的灵活空间，特别是在酒吧和餐厅等私人场所。

《健康增进法》第25条并非简单地要求每个公共场所完全无烟。相反，它鼓励企业采取“适当的被动吸烟预防措施”。在实践中，这为几种例外情况留出了空间。

其中最重要的例外之一是小企业例外。面积在100平方米或以下的餐厅和酒吧——这在东京的社区酒吧中很常见——只要在入口处张贴允许吸烟的标志，就可以允许室内吸烟。没有员工的小企业也可以允许吸烟。

这很重要，因为在日本，小型个体经营场所非常普遍。许多酒吧只有一张吧台、几把凳子和一个人经营整个场所。对其中一些场所来说，喝酒或吃饭时能够吸烟是吸引力的组成部分。

“我个人不喜欢室内吸烟，但我们的酒吧里确实有顾客认为在吧台吸烟是酒吧体验的 essential part（必要组成部分），”在六本木经营一家酒吧的田中Keith说。“与此同时，我们知道其他客人对烟雾感到不适，所以找到适当的平衡并不容易。”

田中补充说，由于“街头吸烟也受到限制”，无法在室内吸烟的顾客“经常不得不离开去一个小的指定吸烟区，这可能会降低满意度并打断体验的连贯性。在实践中，我们尽一切努力负责任地管理环境。我们使用强力通风、配备多台空气循环器和空气净化器，甚至运行大型加湿器，因为空气会变得太干燥。这就是努力平衡舒适度、文化和日常酒吧运营的现实。”

当场所较大或有员工时，吸烟通常在整个空间内被禁止。但业主可以安装符合通风和密封要求的指定吸烟室。任何在东京夜生活区待过的人可能都见过它们：嵌入餐厅或酒吧角落的玻璃墙房间，顾客可以坐在里面喝酒、点餐同时吸烟。

物理空间并非日本法规管理吸烟的唯一方式。规则还对香烟和菲利普莫里斯公司的IQOS或日本烟草公司的Ploom等新型烟草产品加以区分。由于这些产品加热烟草而非燃烧烟草，监管者认为它们侵扰性较小，因此与传统香烟区别对待。因此，一些禁止普通香烟的场所仍然允许加热烟草产品。

这一切可能让人感觉复杂，但 underlying pattern（潜在模式）很简单：在法律框架内，业主仍然可以塑造其场所的氛围。

在最近一次东京之行中，我问一位经营小酒吧的朋友，为什么他的场所允许某些形式的吸烟但不允许其他形式。他的回答很直接：他允许加热烟草产品，因为那是他自己吸的产品。

对顾客来说，在东京选择酒吧通常也意味着选择一种环境。讨厌香烟烟雾的人可以避开它。喜欢边喝边吸烟的人可以寻找允许这样做的场所。

日本曾经比现在更倾向于消费者选择。在2020年的限制之前，在餐厅和酒吧内吸烟要普遍得多。今天这种 patchy exemptions and designated spaces（零散的例外和指定空间）的混合体，在很多方面是早期制度的残余。

这种残余仍然提供了一些有趣的东西。在东京，酒吧内的空气并非完全由政府监管者决定。有时，它是由吧台后面的人以及选择走进门的人共同做出的选择。
articles.summary:
Walk into three different bars in Tokyo, and you may have three completely different experiences: one bar thick with cigarette smoke, one with a sealed glass smoking room humming in the corner, and one entirely smoke-free.

Within regulatory boundaries, the choice often lies with both the owner and the customer: Proprietors decide what kind of space they want to run, and patrons decide which environment they prefer. Compare that to much of the United States, where indoor smoking is broadly prohibited by state or local law. Roughly 82.4 percent of Americans are covered by 100 percent smoke-free rules in workplaces, restaurants, and bars, largely removing the decision from the marketplace.

Like many other countries, Japan regulates smoking heavily. The 2020 revision of the Health Promotion Act introduced major restrictions on indoor smoking in public places such as parks, government buildings, hospitals, and most workplaces.

In many Japanese cities, smoking on the street is restricted as well. You can't simply light a cigarette while standing outside or walking down the sidewalk. Instead, smokers must seek out designated areas—small smoking stations separated by glass or plastic partitions and equipped with ashtrays. Lighting up outside these areas can lead to fines of up to 300,000 yen ($1,890).

At first glance, Japan's system might appear stricter than the rules in the U.S., where indoor smoking is banned but outdoor smoking is generally tolerated. But Japan's laws leave surprising room for flexibility, especially inside private venues such as bars and restaurants.

Article 25 of the Health Promotion Act does not simply mandate that every public place be entirely smoke-free. Instead, it encourages businesses to take "appropriate passive smoking prevention measures." In practice, this leaves room for several exceptions.

One of the most significant is the small-business exception. Restaurants and bars with a floor space of 100 square meters or less—the size of many neighborhood bars in Tokyo—can allow indoor smoking as long as they post a sign at the entrance indicating that smoking is permitted. Small businesses with no employees also can allow smoking.

That matters because small, owner-operated establishments are very common in Japan. Many bars consist of a counter, a handful of stools, and a single person running the entire operation. For some of these venues, the ability to smoke while drinking or eating is part of their appeal.

"I personally don't like smoking inside, but at our bar there are definitely customers who see smoking at the counter as an essential part of the bar experience," says Keith Tanaka, who runs a bar in Roppongi. "At the same time, we know that other guests are uncomfortable with smoke, so finding the right balance is never easy."

Since "smoking on the street is also restricted," Tanaka adds, customers who cannot smoke inside "often have to leave for a small designated smoking area, which can lower satisfaction and break the flow of the experience. In practice, we do everything we can to manage the environment responsibly. We run strong ventilation, use several air circulators and air purifiers, and even operate large humidifiers because the air becomes too dry. That is the reality of trying to balance comfort, culture, and day-to-day bar operations."

When venues are larger or have employees, smoking is generally banned throughout the space. But owners may install designated smoking rooms that meet ventilation and sealing requirements. Anyone who has spent time in Tokyo's nightlife district has likely seen them: glass-walled rooms tucked into the corner of a restaurant or bar where customers can sit, drink, and order food while smoking.

Physical spaces are not the only way Japanese regulations address smoking. The rules also distinguish between cigarettes and newer tobacco products such as Philip Morris' IQOS or Japan Tobacco's Ploom. Because these products heat tobacco rather than burn it, regulators consider them less intrusive and treat them differently from traditional cigarettes. As a result, some venues that prohibit regular cigarettes still allow heated tobacco products.

All of this can feel complicated, but the underlying pattern is simple: Within the legal framework, owners can still shape the atmosphere of their establishments.

During a recent visit to Tokyo, I asked a friend who owns a small bar why his venue allowed some forms of smoking but not others. His answer was straightforward: He allows heated tobacco products because that's what he smokes.

For customers, choosing a bar in Tokyo often means choosing an environment as well. Someone who dislikes cigarette smoke can avoid it. Someone who enjoys smoking with a drink can seek out places where that is permitted.

Japan once leaned further toward consumer choice than it does today. Before the 2020 restrictions, smoking inside restaurants and bars was far more common. Today's patchwork of exemptions and designated spaces is, in many ways, the remnant of that earlier system.

That remnant still offers something interesting. In Tokyo, the air inside a bar is not determined entirely by government regulators. Sometimes it is the choice of the person behind the counter and the people who choose to walk through the door.

The post Japan's Smoking Bans Make a Lot More Sense Than America's appeared first on Reason.com.
